message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: TRAP TO AVOID: web search results attribute a detailed 'Report Structure Planner / Section Formatter / Section Knowledge Agent / Query Generator / Result Accumulator / Reflection Agent / Final Section Formatter / Finalizer' pipeline to Tavily. This is NOT Tavily's architecture -- it is a third-party Medium/TDS tutorial that merely USES the Tavily search API.
DETAIL: The search index blends the Tavily docs with Robert Martin-Short's 'Building a Research Agent That Can Write to Google Docs' (Towards Data Science) and a LangGraph+Tavily Medium tutorial, and the snippet-summarizer presents that tutorial's LangGraph node names as if they were Tavily Research's internal stages. I verified against the raw Tavily docs HTML and Tavily's own skills repo: the ONLY tools Tavily Research emits are Planning, WebSearch, ResearchSubtopic, Generating. There is no 'Reflection Agent' node and no per-section formatter in Tavily Research. If anyone on the team cites a Tavily 'Section Formatter' or 'Reflection Agent', it is contamination from a tutorial.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://towardsdatascience.com/building-a-research-agent-that-can-write-to-google-docs-part-1-4b49ea05a292/
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: List the exact names of the agent nodes/stages/components in this article's pipeline (e.g. Report Structure Planner, Section Formatter, Section Knowledge Agent, Query Generator, Result Accumulator, Reflection Agent, Final Section Formatter, Finalizer). Also state the author and whether it uses Tavily search API, and whether it claims to describe Tavily's own architecture.

input.query: Tavily Research agent tools Planning WebSearch ResearchSubtopic Generating

content.content:
# Research Agent Pipeline Components

**Agent Nodes/Stages:**
1. Initial Plan (Planner)
2. Do Research
3. Write
4. Editor
5. Review
6. Research Revise
7. Accept
8. Reject

**Author:** Robert Martin-Short

**Tavily Search API:** Yes, the system uses Tavily search to retrieve web content for the research phase.

**Tavily Architecture Description:** No. The article states that "Under the hood, Tavily is likely using web scrapers and LLMs to extract content relevant to the user's search, but all of that is abstracted away," but explicitly does not claim to describe Tavily's actual architecture—only speculating about possible internal mechanisms.

Web search results for query: "Tavily Research agent tools Planning WebSearch ResearchSubtopic Generating"

Links: [{"title":"GPT Researcher - Tavily Docs","url":"https://docs.tavily.com/examples/open-sources/gpt-researcher"},{"title":"Build dynamic web research agents with the Strands Agents SDK and Tavily | Artificial Intelligence","url":"https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/build-dynamic-web-research-agents-with-the-strands-agents-sdk-and-tavily/"},{"title":"Tavily Research Agent - IBM Cloud","url":"https://cloud.ibm.com/catalog/services/tavily-research-agent"},{"title":"Hermes Agent Web Search: How to Wire Tavily Into a Self-Improving Agent | Tavily Blog","url":"https://www.tavily.com/blog/hermes-agent-web-search-how-to-wire-tavily-into-a-self-improving-agent"},{"title":"Tavily","url":"https://www.tavily.com/"},{"title":"Tavily Review 2026 | AI Infrastructure & MLOps Tool - Pricing & Features","url":"https://aiagentslist.com/agents/tavily"},{"title":"GitHub - tavily-ai/tavily_company_researcher · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/tavily-ai/tavily_company_researcher"},{"title":"Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art","url":"https://huggingface.co/blog/Tavily/tavily-deep-research"},{"title":"Tavily","url":"https://ai-sdk.dev/resources/tools/tavily"},{"title":"Tavily - Web Search API for AI Agents | EveryDev.ai","url":"https://www.everydev.ai/tools/tavily"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Tavily Research agent tools, Planning, WebSearch, ResearchSubtopic, and Generating:

## Overview

Tavily is the real‑time search engine for AI agents and RAG workflows — Fast and secure APIs for web search and content extraction.

## Planning and Research Architecture

The main idea is to run "planner" and "execution" agents, whereas the planner generates questions to research, and the execution agents seek the most related information based on each generated research question. Finally, the planner filters and aggregates all related information and creates a research report.

## Core Tools and Capabilities

Tavily provides purpose-built APIs for: Search – Retrieve semantically ranked links and content snippets across the public web, filtered by domain, recency, or count · Extract – Pull raw content or cleaned markdown from known URLs for summarization, QA, or embedding · Crawl – Traverse websites recursively through links to simulate exploratory behavior and build site maps.

## Research Subtopic Generation

Generate a set of research questions that together form an objective opinion on any given task. For each research question, trigger a crawler agent that scrapes online resources for information relevant to the given task. For each scraped resources, summarize based on relevant information and keep track of its sources.

## Integration with Agents

Tavily's web_search exposes topic, time_range, country, and include_domains/exclude_domains filters as first-class API fields. The planner can drive freshness or domain scoping directly instead of hacking it into the query string.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.query: "Report Structure Planner" "Section Formatter" "Reflection Agent" "Query Generator" LangGraph research agent

input.prompt: What are the internal stages/tools of Tavily's deep research agent? Does it list tools named Planning, WebSearch, ResearchSubtopic, Generating? Is there a Reflection step or per-section formatter? Also, what does it report on DeepResearch Bench (RACE score, judge model, date)?

content.content:
# Tavily's Deep Research Agent Details

Based on the article provided, **specific internal stages and tool names are not explicitly listed**. The document does not mention tools named "Planning," "WebSearch," "ResearchSubtopic," or "Generating," nor does it describe a dedicated Reflection step or per-section formatter.

## What the Article Does Reveal

The document emphasizes architectural principles rather than specific tool names:

- **Context Engineering Focus**: The agent uses "context-managed web retrieval" with deduplication and global state persistence to avoid overfitting to single research threads.

- **Minimal Toolset Philosophy**: "it's better to expose a small, essential toolset to the agent rather than a large, complex one" to reduce failure modes.

- **Distillation Process**: Tool outputs are described as being "distilled into reflections," with only past reflections used as context for subsequent iterations—this differs from traditional ReAct architectures.

## DeepResearch Bench Reporting

The article mentions achieving "SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" and reducing token consumption by 66% compared to Open Deep Research, but **does not specify which metrics (RACE score, judge model, or benchmark date) are reported** on the leaderboard itself.

For complete technical specifications, you would need to consult Tavily's API documentation or the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard directly.

Web search results for query: ""Report Structure Planner" "Section Formatter" "Reflection Agent" "Query Generator" LangGraph research agent"

Links: [{"title":"Deep Research AI Workflow Using Langgraph + Tavily + Any LLM Provider | by Gaurav Sharma | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@gaurav219688/deep-research-ai-workflow-using-langgraph-tavily-search-any-llm-provider-373ae5aa2cfd"},{"title":"Workflows and agents - Docs by LangChain","url":"https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/workflows-agents"},{"title":"“Delving” into LangGraph. (Building a sales report generating… | by Anukriti Ranjan | Medium","url":"https://anukriti-ranjan.medium.com/delving-into-langgraph-3b02f4d05b4c"},{"title":"LangChain/LangGraph: Build Reflection Enabled Agentic | by TeeTracker | Medium","url":"https://teetracker.medium.com/build-reflection-enabled-agent-9186a35c6581"},{"title":"Building Your Own AI Agent: A Practical Guide with LangGraph - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/midas126/building-your-own-ai-agent-a-practical-guide-with-langgraph-4f2l"},{"title":"Building a Reflection Agent Using LangGraph: A Beginner-Friendly Guide | by Ashish Malhotra | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@mrcoffeeai/building-a-reflection-agent-using-langgraph-a-beginner-friendly-guide-33f7772d5eae"},{"title":"LangGraph 101: Let’s Build A Deep Research Agent | Towards Data Science","url":"https://towardsdatascience.com/langgraph-101-lets-build-a-deep-research-agent/"},{"title":"LangGraph in 2026: Build Multi-Agent AI Systems That Actually Work - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/ottoaria/langgraph-in-2026-build-multi-agent-ai-systems-that-actually-work-3h5"},{"title":"Build Multi Agent AI Research App with LangGraph | Level Up Coding","url":"https://levelup.gitconnected.com/build-deep-research-planning-multi-agent-ai-app-from-scratch-35016343b8e3"},{"title":"Build an intelligent financial analysis agent with LangGraph and Strands Agents | Artificial Intelligence","url":"https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/build-an-intelligent-financial-analysis-agent-with-langgraph-and-strands-agents/"}]

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the research agent workflow you're asking about. This appears to be from a "Deep Research AI Workflow" implementation using LangGraph.

## Key Components Found:

The specialized AI agents include:
- **Report Structure Planner**: Creates the initial report structure based on the user's topic and outline
- **Section Formatter**: Breaks down the approved report structure into discrete sections for processing
- **Query Generator**: Creates targeted search queries based on the section content and existing knowledge
- **Reflection Agent**: Evaluates the quality and completeness of the accumulated content, determining whether additional research is needed

## Workflow Architecture:

Each agent is implemented as a node in the LangGraph workflow, with clear inputs, outputs, and responsibilities. The workflow's strength lies in its combination of broad structure planning with deep, section-specific research.

## Additional Components:

The system also includes a Final Section Formatter (which creates polished, well-structured content for each section) and a Finalizer (which combines all sections, generates a conclusion and references, and produces the final report).

## Human Feedback Integration:

Human feedback is integrated at a critical juncture after the initial report structure is generated but before the detailed research begins. If feedback is provided, the workflow loops back to the Report Structure Planner with the user's input, allowing the planner to incorporate the feedback and generate a revised structure.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the Tavily Research API streaming functionality.

In Pro mode, the Research API supports ResearchSubtopic tool_call/response cycles for deeper research, along with stream events including Generating tool_call, Generating final report, Generating tool_response, Report generated, Content events (streamed report chunks), Sources event (complete list of all sources used), and Done event (stream complete).

The first search result comes directly from the Tavily documentation page on streaming (docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming), which contains information about how the ResearchSubtopic tool is used in the context of the Research API's streaming events.

input.prompt: List ALL tool names emitted in the stream (tool_call names) for Tavily Research, in fast mode and pro mode. Quote them exactly. Is there any Reflection tool or Section Formatter?

content.content:
# Tavily Research Tool Names

Based on the documentation provided, here are all tool names emitted in the stream:

**Both Modes (mini and pro):**
- `"Planning"` - "Initializes the research plan based on the input query"
- `"WebSearch"` - "Executes web searches to gather information"
- `"Generating"` - "Generates the final research report from collected information"

**Pro Mode Only:**
- `"ResearchSubtopic"` - "Conducts deep research on specific subtopics"

## Reflection and Section Formatter Tools

No, there is no mention of a Reflection tool or Section Formatter tool in the provided documentation. The documentation explicitly lists only the four tools above in its "Tool Types" section.

content.text:
Verified against primary sources.

**Tavily's actual Research tool list (primary source: https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming):** The "Tool Types" section lists exactly four tool_call names — `Planning` ("Initializes the research plan based on the input query"), `WebSearch` ("Executes web searches to gather information"), `Generating` ("Generates the final research report from collected information"), and `ResearchSubtopic` ("Conducts deep research on specific subtopics", **pro mode only**). There is no Reflection tool and no per-section formatter. The claim's core warning is correct.

**But the source attribution in the claim is wrong.** I fetched the given URL (Robert Martin-Short, "Building a Research Agent That Can Write to Google Docs", TDS). Its LangGraph nodes are: Initial Plan / Do Research / Write / Editor / Review / Research Revise / Accept / Reject. It contains none of the eight names in the claim, and it explicitly disclaims knowledge of Tavily's internals ("Under the hood, Tavily is likely using web scrapers and LLMs... but all of that is abstracted away").

The actual origin of the contaminating node names is a *different* article: Gaurav Sharma, "Deep Research AI Workflow Using Langgraph + Tavily Search + Any LLM Provider" (Medium) — https://medium.com/@gaurav219688/deep-research-ai-workflow-using-langgraph-tavily-search-any-llm-provider-373ae5aa2cfd — which defines Report Structure Planner, Section Formatter, Query Generator, Reflection Agent, Final Section Formatter, and Finalizer verbatim.

**Currency note:** the claim makes no DeepResearch Bench score assertion, so the 2026 leaderboard/judge change (Gemini-2.5-Pro → GPT-5.5 as RACE judge) doesn't invalidate it. Related caution: Tavily's own HF blog ("Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art") claims SOTA on DeepResearch Bench but does not name the judge model or date, so any Tavily RACE number pulled from it is unverifiable as-is and must not be quoted as current.

input.reasoning:
The operative warning is CONFIRMED against a primary source, but one supporting detail in the claim is false.

CONFIRMED (primary source: Tavily's own docs, https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming): the "Tool Types" section of the Research streaming reference enumerates exactly four tool_call names — Planning ("Initializes the research plan based on the input query"), WebSearch ("Executes web searches to gather information"), Generating ("Generates the final research report from collected information"), plus ResearchSubtopic ("Conducts deep research on specific subtopics") in pro mode only. There is NO Reflection Agent, NO Section Formatter, NO Query Generator, NO Finalizer. So the claim's core assertion — that a "Report Structure Planner / Section Formatter / Section Knowledge Agent / Query Generator / Result Accumulator / Reflection Agent / Final Section Formatter / Finalizer" pipeline is NOT Tavily Research's architecture — is correct and independently reproducible from Tavily's primary docs. Tavily's own HF engineering blog ("Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art") likewise names none of those stages and stresses a deliberately minimal toolset.

FALSE SUB-DETAIL (source misattribution): I fetched the SOURCE GIVEN, Robert Martin-Short's "Building a Research Agent That Can Write to Google Docs" (Towards Data Science). Its LangGraph nodes are Initial Plan / Do Research / Write / Editor / Review / Research Revise / Accept / Reject. It contains none of the eight names in the claim, and it explicitly disclaims knowledge of Tavily internals ("Under the hood, Tavily is likely using web scrapers and LLMs to extract content relevant to the user's search, but all of that is abstracted away"). It is therefore not a contamination source for those names. The true origin is a different tutorial: Gaurav Sharma, "Deep Research AI Workflow Using Langgraph + Tavily Search + Any LLM Provider" (Medium), which defines Report Structure Planner, Section Formatter, Query Generator, Reflection Agent, Final Section Formatter and Finalizer verbatim as LangGraph nodes.

CURRENCY: the claim asserts no DeepResearch Bench number, so the 2026 leaderboard/evaluator change (Gemini-2.5-Pro replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge) does not affect it. Adjacent caution for the team: Tavily's HF blog claims "SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" without naming the judge model or a date, so any Tavily RACE score sourced from it is stale/unverifiable under the new judge and must not be quoted as current.

NET: keep the warning (do not cite a Tavily "Section Formatter" or "Reflection Agent"), but fix the citation — cite the Tavily streaming docs as the positive evidence and the Gaurav Sharma Medium article as the contamination source, not the TDS Google Docs article.
input.corrected: Tavily Research's only emitted tools are Planning, WebSearch, Generating (all modes) and ResearchSubtopic (pro mode only) — no Reflection Agent, no Section Formatter. Source: https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming ("Tool Types"). The contaminating node names (Report Structure Planner, Section Formatter, Query Generator, Reflection Agent, Final Section Formatter, Finalizer) come from Gaurav Sharma, "Deep Research AI Workflow Using Langgraph + Tavily Search + Any LLM Provider" (Medium, https://medium.com/@gaurav219688/deep-research-ai-workflow-using-langgraph-tavily-search-any-llm-provider-373ae5aa2cfd) — NOT from Robert Martin-Short's TDS "Building a Research Agent That Can Write to Google Docs" (whose nodes are Initial Plan / Do Research / Write / Editor / Review / Research Revise / Accept / Reject).
